channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Can't keep a habit? This comic shares a proven formula to make it stick
item.description: In this illustrated guide, behavioral scientist BJ Fogg breaks down his Tiny Habits framework to help you rethink your approach to starting (or restarting) a habit.
item.content:encoded: In this illustrated guide, behavioral scientist BJ Fogg breaks down his Tiny Habits framework to help you rethink your approach to starting (or restarting) a habit.
item.dc:creator: Marielle Segarra

item.title: To revive an extinct bird, you first need an artificial egg
item.description: Colossal Biosciences, a Texas company trying to bring extinct species back to life, reports creating artificial eggs that would be necessary to revive extinct birds such as the dodo.
item.content:encoded:
Colossal Biosciences, a Texas company trying to bring extinct species back to life, reports creating artificial eggs that would be necessary to revive extinct birds such as the dodo.

(Image credit: Colossal Biosciences)
item.dc:creator: Rob Stein

item.title: Some plants have a genetic superpower that may help them survive a cataclysm
item.description: Get ready for a biology lesson. Certain plants have extra sets of chromosomes. And it turns out, it's a useful trait for a species facing a dramatic event like climate change.
item.content:encoded:
Get ready for a biology lesson. Certain plants have extra sets of chromosomes. And it turns out, it's a useful trait for a species facing a dramatic event like climate change.

(Image credit: Wolfgang Kaehler/LightRocket)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Thousands of U.S. countertop workers could have damaged lungs, safety expert says
item.description: Over 550 men in California have fallen ill after cutting natural or factory-made stone countertops. But epidemiologists say this isn't just a California problem.
item.content:encoded:
Over 550 men in California have fallen ill after cutting natural or factory-made stone countertops. But epidemiologists say this isn't just a California problem.

(Image credit: Tina Russell for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Nell Greenfieldboyce

item.title: It takes a village – or a Phoenix suburb – to wrangle a wayward tortoise
item.description: When a large tortoise named Rex got loose, a Phoenix-area neighborhood went into a tizzy. More than just a fun commotion, Rex's daring getaway shows the challenges of sulcata tortoise ownership.
item.content:encoded:
When a large tortoise named Rex got loose, a Phoenix-area neighborhood went into a tizzy. More than just a fun commotion, Rex's daring getaway shows the challenges of sulcata tortoise ownership.

(Image credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Alina Hartounian

item.title: What scientists are learning about geoengineering from satellite pollution
item.description: Professor Eloise Marais from the University College London talks about her research on pollution from satellites and its impact on earth's climate.
item.content:encoded: Professor Eloise Marais from the University College London talks about her research on pollution from satellites and its impact on earth's climate.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: A surprising solution to cut asphalt's impact on human health
item.description: Elham Fini, professor of Sustainable Engineering and the Built Environment at Arizona State University, talks about her work on the health impact of asphalt emissions and a solution that could minimize the harmful effects.
item.content:encoded: Elham Fini, professor of Sustainable Engineering and the Built Environment at Arizona State University, talks about her work on the health impact of asphalt emissions and a solution that could minimize the harmful effects.
item.dc:creator: Tinbete Ermyas

item.title: World Health Organization declares Ebola outbreak in Congo a global health emergency
item.description: Health officials are working to contain a new Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. There's no approved vaccine for the strain of the virus that's spreading.
item.content:encoded:
Health officials are working to contain a new Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo. There's no approved vaccine for the strain of the virus that's spreading.

(Image credit: Constant Same Bagalwa)
item.dc:creator: Sydney Lupkin

item.title: This week in science: Sunscreen from fish, art and aging, and a sustainable marimba
item.description: NPR's science podcast Short Wave brings us the stories of how future sunscreens might come from fish, how loving art may impact aging, and a student's quest to build a more sustainable marimba.
item.content:encoded: NPR's science podcast Short Wave brings us the stories of how future sunscreens might come from fish, how loving art may impact aging, and a student's quest to build a more sustainable marimba.
item.dc:creator: Regina G. Barber

item.title: Some plants have unusual genetics, which can help them weather cataclysmic events
item.description: Unlike humans, many plants have more than two sets of chromosomes. This trait may help them adapt to environmental upheaval, such as climate change.
item.content:encoded: Unlike humans, many plants have more than two sets of chromosomes. This trait may help them adapt to environmental upheaval, such as climate change.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
